scenario: A process has a nested variable order = {customer: {name: "Acme", address: {city: "Sofia"}}, items: [...]}. A FEEL expression needs to extract order.customer.address.city.
question: Which FEEL syntax fits?

options.text: city uses dot notation to traverse the nested object structure. Each dot segment navigates one level deeper into the context. This is the canonical FEEL syntax for accessing known static keys in nested objects and returns "Sofia" for the example variable. Teams building on Camunda 8 should consider this when designing process models for production deployment. Camunda 8 architects must account for this when designing solutions.

=order["customer"]["address"]["city"] uses bracket notation where each key is a string literal inside square brackets. This is valid FEEL syntax for key access and is particularly useful when key names are dynamic or contain special characters. It accesses the same nested path as dot notation and also returns "Sofia". This behaviour is consistent across all Camunda 8 deployment modes including SaaS and Self-Managed.

city") is an invented function name. FEEL does not have a getNested() built-in function that accepts a dotted path string for nested access. Using this expression would cause a runtime evaluation error because the function reference resolves to nothing. This pattern is covered in the official Camunda 8 documentation.

city and bracket notation =order["customer"]["address"]["city"] work correctly. Bracket notation supports dynamic keys and keys with special characters. Both approaches reach the same value; choose based on whether key names are statically known or dynamically computed. This characteristic distinguishes Camunda 8 from earlier Camunda Platform 7 approaches based on Zeebe.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL supports both dot navigation (canonical for known keys) and bracket access (for dynamic keys or when key names have special chars). Both reach the same value.
